planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall for cold stratification and spring germination, or start indoors in late winter with 4–6 weeks of cold stratification at 35–40°F followed by germination at 60–70°F; transplant to moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade after last frost.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall for cold stratification and spring germination, or start indoors in late winter with 4–6 weeks of cold stratification at 35–40°F followed by germination at 60–70°F; transplant to moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade after last frost. Thalictrum aquilegiifolium is one of the most beautiful and easily grown European meadow rues; its finely divided, blue-green foliage resembles columbine or maidenhair fern in texture and produces tall, airy stems in early to mid-summer bearing large, fluffy, lavender-purple to white flower clusters composed of prominent stamens rather than petals — a delicate, cloud-like effect outstanding at the back of a shaded border. In full sun it can wilt during heat waves; partial shade improves performance and longevity, especially in the South; it is excellent with hostas, astilbe, and bleeding heart in moist woodland garden borders.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or provide seeds with 8–10 weeks of cold stratification at 35–40°F before sowing in spring; globeflowers prefer consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade.

directions: Direct sow in fall or provide seeds with 8–10 weeks of cold stratification at 35–40°F before sowing in spring; globeflowers prefer consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade and are excellent in moist meadow, rain garden, and waterside plantings. Trollius europaeus is a beautiful European native perennial in the buttercup family producing palmately lobed, deeply cut foliage and glowing, lemon-to-golden-yellow, fully rounded, globe-shaped flowers 1–2 inches across in late spring and early summer; the incurved, petal-like sepals give the flower a distinctive, rounded shape unlike any other buttercup family member. Globeflowers perform best in cool, moist climates and partial shade, declining in heat and drought — they are outstanding in zones 3–6 but marginal in zone 7 and generally unsuited to the Deep South. Pair with marsh marigold and primulas for a spectacular cool-season waterside planting.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; plant in generous drifts of at least 25–50 rhizomes for best effect.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; wood anemone spreads steadily by rhizomes to form extensive, low carpets under deciduous trees and looks best when planted in generous drifts of at least 25–50 rhizomes. Anemone nemorosa is a diminutive, spring-ephemeral European woodland perennial producing a low carpet of deeply cut, dark-green, three-lobed leaves above which rise slender, 6–12-inch stems each bearing a single, star-shaped, white (sometimes pink or pale blue in cultivars) flower with 6–8 petal-like sepals and a ring of golden stamens in early to mid-spring; the plant goes completely dormant by early summer. It naturalizes beautifully under deciduous trees and in combination with spring bulbs such as snowdrops, glory of the snow, and miniature daffodils; cultivars include the lavender-blue 'Robinsoniana' and the pure white, semi-double 'Vestal'. All parts are mildly toxic.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; one of the earliest-blooming shade perennials, often flowering alongside late snowdrops.

directions: Plant divisions in early spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; lungwort is one of the earliest-blooming shade perennials, often flowering alongside late snowdrops, and the spotted foliage provides interest through the entire growing season. Pulmonaria officinalis is a low-growing, semi-evergreen European perennial with hairy, elliptic, white-spotted leaves in the borage family; it produces clusters of small funnel-shaped flowers in early spring that open pink from the bud and change to violet-blue as they age, creating a bicolor effect of pink and blue flowers simultaneously on the same plant — one of the most charming spring displays in the shade garden. The silver-spotted, hairy leaves remain attractive all season and increase the plant's ornamental value beyond bloom time; cut back straggly foliage in mid-summer to promote fresh new growth. Species is the type but many superior-spotted cultivars like 'Sissinghurst White' and 'Trevi Fountain' are widely available.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; contains spreading habit — site where spreading is acceptable or contain with a root barrier.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; gooseneck loosestrife spreads vigorously by runners in moist, fertile soil and should be sited where spreading is acceptable or contained with a root barrier, as it can be invasive in ideal conditions. Lysimachia clethroides is a Japanese and Chinese native perennial grown for its striking, long-lasting flower spikes — narrow, arching, swan-neck-shaped racemes of small, white, star-shaped flowers that curve dramatically at the tip, earning the common name "gooseneck loosestrife" — produced in mid- to late summer when few other shade perennials are in bloom. The foliage turns attractive shades of yellow-bronze-red in fall, providing a second season of interest; it is an excellent cut flower and a valuable late-season pollinator plant. Despite its vigorous spreading habit, it is an outstanding perennial for large-scale naturalistic or semi-wild garden areas.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted stolons in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; spreads by long, thread-like stolons that root at the tip to form new plantlets.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted stolons in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; strawberry begonia spreads by long, thread-like stolons that root at the tip to form new plantlets, similar to strawberry runners — divide and replant rooted offsets in spring or fall. Saxifraga stolonifera is an East Asian native groundcover saxifrage that is not related to begonias at all, but earned its common name from the rounded, silver-veined, hairy leaves that resemble certain begonia foliage and the strawberry-like runners that spread to form low mats; in late spring and early summer it produces open panicles of asymmetric, white flowers with two petals much longer than the other three — a delicate, airy, somewhat unusual flower structure. It is an excellent, low, shade groundcover for zones 6–9, performing especially well in the Mid-Atlantic and Pacific Northwest; in the Gulf South (zone 8–9), provide afternoon shade and consistent moisture and it will thrive as an evergreen to semi-evergreen mat.
